ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general satay entry; no pork-specific satay with peanut sauce entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork meat products — grilled/barbecued pork preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated pork satay with peanut sauce code. A0CKM (pork meat products) used as closest defensible anchor; peanut sauce component not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pork satay with peanut sauce as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pork satay is 4 skewers (approximately 80–100g of meat) accompanied by 2–3 tablespoons of peanut sauce, totalling approximately 120g, commonly served at hawker centres with compressed rice (ketupat) and cucumber slices.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Satay with Sauce — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists 'Pork satay with sauce' (F06100038) as a canonical entry with per-100g nutrient values. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken directly from this record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork loin grilled profile (NDB 10062) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; value cross-validated against typical pork satay fatty acid composition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat-and-peanut-sauce dish with moderate carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for pork satay with peanut sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) peanut sauce GI ~14 (peanuts, University of Sydney GI Database); (2) marinated pork with spices contributing negligible carbohydrate GI impact; (3) sugar in marinade and sauce raising effective GI toward low-medium boundary. Composite dish GI estimated at 45 (low). Glycemic load per 120g serving = (45 × 14.44g carbohydrate × 1.2) / 100 = 7.8g. This estimate is conservative and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 386mg/100g combined with high sugar 13.12g/100g and saturated fat; peanut sauce adds further sodium and sugar load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit pork satay to 2–3 skewers per sitting, request peanut sauce on the side and use no more than one tablespoon, and avoid additional condiments such as soy sauce or sambal to keep total sodium intake within the 2000mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 13.12g/100g predominantly from peanut sauce; moderate carbohydrate 14.44g/100g with GI 45 (low) partially mitigated by fat and protein slowing gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 3–4 skewers without ketupat, request peanut sauce sparingly or on the side, and monitor postprandial glucose as the concentrated sugar in the sauce can elevate blood glucose despite the overall low GI of the dish.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~5.8g/100g from pork and peanut sauce, trans fat 0.1g/100g from charcoal grilling, sodium 386mg/100g, and high sugar 13.12g/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict pork satay to an occasional treat of no more than 3 skewers, choose leaner cuts where possible, avoid the peanut sauce or use a minimal amount, and pair with non-starchy vegetables rather than ketupat to reduce the overall atherogenic and glycaemic burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifying TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; charcoal grilling produces advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); sugar 13.12g/100g promotes inflammatory mediator production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit pork satay due to its pro-inflammatory fatty acid profile, AGE content from charcoal grilling, and high sugar load; chicken satay with reduced-sugar sauce is a lower-arachidonic-acid alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Satay with Peanut Sauce is a popular Singaporean and Malay hawker dish consisting of boneless pork pieces marinated in a spiced mixture of turmeric, coriander, cumin, lemongrass, sugar, and soy sauce, threaded onto bamboo skewers and charcoal-grilled until lightly charred and fragrant. The dish is served with a rich, sweet-savoury peanut sauce made from ground roasted peanuts, coconut milk, palm sugar, tamarind, and chilli, alongside compressed rice cakes (ketupat) and fresh cucumber and onion slices. The pork is tender and smoky with a caramelised exterior from the sugar in the marinade. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 268 kcal, 14.9g protein, 16.67g fat (of which approximately 5.8g is saturated), 14.44g carbohydrate, and 13.12g sugar — the high sugar content is largely attributable to the peanut sauce. Sodium is 386mg/100g, placing it in the borderline-moderate range for hypertension. Dietary fibre is 2.5g/100g. The glycemic index is estimated at 45 (low), though the concentrated sugar in the peanut sauce can elevate postprandial glucose in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not halal due to the use of pork. It is a high-protein, moderate-fat hawker staple commonly consumed as a dinner or snack at Singapore hawker centres and food courts, and is culturally associated with Malay cuisine and festive occasions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pork satay with peanut sauce?
Is pork satay high in sodium?
What is the GI of pork satay?
How much protein in pork satay?
Is satay babi suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical record F06100038 with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork grilled profile as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; this is the only adapted value. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database peanut and mixed-dish data. All other values are exact from HPB.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for pork satay with peanut sauce is not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central composite dish entries as of 2026. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo record F06100038 and no sufficiently close composite entry exists in USDA FoodData Central for pork satay with peanut sauce as of 2026. trans_fat_g set to 0.1: trace trans fat from charcoal grilling; value is an estimate consistent with grilled pork literature. Saturated fat 5.8g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 10062 (pork loin, grilled) and cross-validated against typical pork satay fatty acid profiles; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for pork satay with peanut sauce. The dietary_tags field in the input included 'plant_based' which is incorrect for a pork dish and has been removed. The input condition key 'diabetes' has been remapped to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules.
